figures.caption: Table 1: Metric evaluation results (mIoU ) for object instance segmentation as a function of the number of clicks: top, trained and tested on ScanNet40 [5]; bottom, trained and tested on KITTI360 [32].
Table 2: Evaluation of instance segmentation using mIoU, mAP@0.25, and mAP@0.5 on ScanNet40 [5] (left) and KITTI360 [1], showing full metric results.

Fig. 6: Plot of (a) mIoU as a function of embedding dimension, with spatial embeddings in blue and semantic embeddings in orange; (b) mIoU as a function of the number of semantic class prototype embedding.

Fig. 2: Baseline comparison at 1 click per instance with identical click positions: above the dashed line on ScanNet40 [5], and below on KITTI360 [32]. Each instance class is shown using a consistent color, with the red box showing a zoomed-in region for closer inspection of the segmentation mask details.
Fig. 4: Plot of mIoU results for all methods as a function of the number of clicks.
Fig. 5: Plot of mIoU test performance on ScanNet40 as a function of the number of click query points during inference (The query numbers ranging from 50 to 200 during training are explored).
Table 4: Ablation study on the main modules of our model, evaluated on the ScanNet40 [5] test split.

Fig. 3: Ablation study visualization of instance segmentation on a selected indoor scene with different modules removed; the leftmost shows the Ground Truth, with stars indicating click point positions as input.

Fig. 1: Overview of our click-based instance segmentation framework. Given a scene S with user-provided clicks C, the scene encoder extracts multi-scale scene features {F0, ...,FL}, while the query encoder produces query features Q. The transformer block refines these features into Qt, which the Conditioned Query Adaptor further refines into Qs using the semantic prototype Ps and semantic embedding Es. The mask decoder employs a mask head and classification head to generate instance embeddings Ei, producing instance masks M. The segmentation is optimized with mask loss Lmask and classification loss Lcls. The dashed line indicates the initial query feature values at the first time step.

Table 3: Quantitative results on interactive multi-object segmentation. For a fair comparison, some interactive single-object segmentation approaches are adapted to our multi-object evaluation setting. These baseline methods continue to generate binary masks for individual objects, and the resulting masks are manually combined to obtain the final multi-object predictions, which follow a similar processing strategy to that proposed in Agile3D [55].
